What Factors Make Horror Movie Successful?

Author

Minavanh CHIRDCHUNG

Setting up

library(tidyverse)
library(gt)
library(scales)
library(dplyr)

horror <- readr::read_csv("horror_movies.csv")

Introduction

Personally, I really enjoy watching horror movies with my friends. The feelings of fear, suspense, and psychological tension are exciting and different from what I get from other genres like romance, drama, or fantasy. What makes it even better is watching together, especially during jump scares when everyone reacts at the same time. Those shared moments make the experience more fun and memorable. I remember when I was living in a boarding house in high school, my friends and I even had a monthly Friday horror movie night.

One of our all-time favorite horror movies is Talk to Me by A24. I really like its unique concept of using a mysterious severed, embalmed hand to communicate with spirits. It also shows how people become addicted to that experience, which feels similar to drug addiction. That idea made the movie more meaningful and interesting to me. Talk to Me is considered part of a new generation of horror films and was described as a very creepy modern horror built on classic ideas by Rotten Tomatoes’s critics consensus. It received high ratings, with 94% on the Rotten Tomatoes Tomatometer and 83% from audiences. It was also very successful financially, earning about 47.6 million USD from a 4.5 million USD budget, making it one of A24’s most successful films.1

The movie also won Best Horror Film at the Saturn Awards in 2024, along with many other nominations and wins.2 All of this made me reflect on how horror movies have remained one of the most popular and long-lasting genres in the entertainment industry. At the same time, movie preferences are very subjective. For example, I am part of the 94% who rated Talk to Me highly, but others might not enjoy it as much. This made me curious about what actually makes a horror movie successful, so I came up with the research question of “What Factors Make Horror Movie Successful?”. Even though horror films are popular, there is often a gap between what people watch and what they consider to be good movies. This project explores whether popular horror movies are also highly rated, or if their success is influenced by other factors like rating, budgeting, marketing or shock value.

Data Cleaning and Preparation

First, I begin with cleaning the data set by removing movies with missing or zero values for budget and revenue. I have noticed that some movies, included Talk to Me, have their budget or revenue recorded as 0, which likely indicates missing data rather than actual values. So, to ensure a more accurate analysis, these movies will be excluded when examining relationships involving budget and revenue. However, they will still be included in other parts of the analysis where those variables are not required.

horror_clean <- horror |>
  filter(budget > 0,
         revenue > 0)

Analysis

Observation 1. Budget vs Revenue

First, let’s take a look at the top 10 movies by budget and by revenue to get an idea of their financial range.

Budget

top_budget <- horror_clean |> 
  arrange(desc(budget)) |>
  select(title,
         budget,
         revenue) |> 
  head (n = 10)

gt(top_budget)
title budget revenue
World War Z 2.00e+08 531865000
Messengers 2.00e+08 5224221
Van Helsing 1.60e+08 300257475
The Meg 1.50e+08 530243742
The Mummy 1.25e+08 409231607
Constantine 1.00e+08 230884728
Sleepy Hollow 1.00e+08 206071502
End of Days 1.00e+08 211989043
What Lies Beneath 1.00e+08 291420351
Alien: Covenant 9.70e+07 240891763

From table above, we noticed that the budget value was shown as “2e+08” or revenue value which was shown as an unformatted numeric value of “531865000”. These can be difficult to read. So, to make the table clearer, I looked up ways to format numeric data in gt and found the function fmt_currency().3

top_budget <- horror_clean |> 
  arrange(desc(budget)) |> 
  dplyr::select(title, budget, revenue) |> 
  head(n = 10) |>
  gt() |> 
  fmt_currency(columns = budget,
               currency = "USD") |>
  fmt_currency(columns = revenue,
               currency = "USD")

top_budget
title budget revenue
World War Z $200,000,000.00 $531,865,000.00
Messengers $200,000,000.00 $5,224,221.00
Van Helsing $160,000,000.00 $300,257,475.00
The Meg $150,000,000.00 $530,243,742.00
The Mummy $125,000,000.00 $409,231,607.00
Constantine $100,000,000.00 $230,884,728.00
Sleepy Hollow $100,000,000.00 $206,071,502.00
End of Days $100,000,000.00 $211,989,043.00
What Lies Beneath $100,000,000.00 $291,420,351.00
Alien: Covenant $97,000,000.00 $240,891,763.00

From the table above, we see that the highest budget movie is World War Z with the total budget of 200,000,000.00 USD and 531,865,000.00 USD. In contrast, Messengers also had the same budget of 200,000,000.00 USD, but only made 5,224,221.00 USD. This shows the sign that having the highest budget does not always guarantee the most profit.

Revenue

top_revenue <- horror_clean |> 
  arrange(desc(revenue)) |> 
  dplyr::select(title, revenue, budget) |> 
  head(n = 10) |>
  gt() |> 
  fmt_currency(columns = revenue,
               currency = "USD") |>
  fmt_currency(columns = budget,
               currency = "USD")
  
top_revenue
title revenue budget
It $701,842,551.00 $35,000,000.00
World War Z $531,865,000.00 $200,000,000.00
The Meg $530,243,742.00 $150,000,000.00
It Chapter Two $473,122,525.00 $79,000,000.00
Jaws $470,653,000.00 $7,000,000.00
The Exorcist $441,306,145.00 $11,000,000.00
The Mummy $409,231,607.00 $125,000,000.00
The Nun $365,550,119.00 $22,000,000.00
A Quiet Place $340,677,200.00 $17,000,000.00
Black Swan $329,398,046.00 $13,000,000.00

In this table, we can see that It had the highest revenue of 701,842,551.00 USD with the budget of 35,000,000 USD. Following by World War Z which was the highest budget movie in the previous table and now ranks as second in revenue. This data further emphasizes the observation point.

horror_clean |>
  mutate(budget_group = if_else(budget > mean(budget, 
                                              na.rm = TRUE),
                                "High",
                                "Low")) |>
  group_by(budget_group) |>
  summarize(avg_revenue = mean(revenue, 
                               na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_currency(columns = avg_revenue,
               currency = "USD")
budget_group avg_revenue
High $82,806,380.54
Low $19,630,098.14

Here, I want to quickly summarize the budget and revenue value, so I use if_else() to split movies into “High” or “Low” budget groups based on whether their budget is above or below the average.

Budget vs Revenue Plot

ggplot(horror_clean,
       aes(x = budget,
           y = revenue)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Budget vs Revenue in Horror Movies",
       x = "Budget",
       y = "Revenue") +
  theme_minimal()

As we can see from the plot above, there is a positive relationship between budget and revenue. This means that movies with higher budgets tend to generate more revenue, as they can invest more in well-known actors, better production quality, and stronger marketing strategies, such as advertising on large platforms like YouTube. However, the points are quite spread out, which suggests that having a bigger budget does not always guarantee financial success.

The two tables above plus a low budget movie like Talk to Me is a really good example to support this observation. While Talk to Me had a relatively small budget, it was highly profitable. This shows that creativity and storytelling can be as important as financial investment.

I also noticed here that the values on both the x-axis and y-axis are shown in “e+08”. To make the graph clearer, I looked up ways to adjust the scale for continuous data and found the functions scale_x_continuous() and scale_y_continuous().4 With the use of these functions, I got this new plot:

ggplot(horror_clean,
       aes(x = budget,
           y = revenue)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::label_dollar()) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_dollar()) +
  labs(title = "Budget vs Revenue in Horror Movies",
       x = "Budget",
       y = "Revenue") +
  theme_minimal()

Observation 2. Popularity vs Rating

Top Rated Horror Movies

As mentioned earlier, movie preferences are very subjective. However, they still play an important role in influencing audience decisions. So, let’s take a look at the top rated horror movies.

Notice. Since I will not be using the budget and revenue variables for this part, I will use the original horror data set instead of horror_clean.

top_rated <- horror |>
  filter(vote_count > 100) |>
  arrange(desc(vote_average)) |>
  select(title, 
         vote_average, 
         vote_count, 
         popularity) |>
  head(n = 10)

gt(top_rated)
title vote_average vote_count popularity
Psycho 8.4 8452 42.064
The Shining 8.2 14911 63.472
Abraham Lincoln Vampire Hunter: The Great Calamity 8.2 200 5.071
Straight Outta Nowhere: Scooby-Doo! Meets Courage the Cowardly Dog 8.1 318 103.618
Alien 8.1 12083 60.982
Michael Jackson's Thriller 8.1 456 10.242
Ghosts 8.1 194 8.230
The Thing 8.0 5566 56.545
What Ever Happened to Baby Jane? 8.0 827 17.940
The Black Phone 7.9 2736 1071.398

We can see that the highest rated movie, Psycho, has a reasonable number of votes of 8,452 votes. However, some movies have very high ratings but only a small number of votes or low popularity, for example, Abraham Lincoln Vampire Hunter: The Great Calamity, which has 8.2 rate but only has around 200 votes and 5.071 popularity. This shows that the highest rating doesn’t always mean a movie is the most popular. It also makes me wonder how popularity affects ratings. I tried setting a minimum of 100 votes, but that excluded some movies and caused a loss of data. However, keeping all movies could also affect the results. So, I decided to explore this further by looking at the relationship between rating and popularity.

Popularity vs Rating Plot

ggplot(horror,
       aes(x = popularity,
           y = vote_average)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(title = "Popularity vs Rating",
       x = "Popularity",
       y = "Average Rating") +
  theme_minimal()

From the plot above, we can see that most movies are clustered at low popularity values, while only a few have extremely high popularity. On the other hand, ratings vary widely across all levels of popularity. Also, the plot clearly shows that there is almost no a relationship between popularity and rating.

Observation 3. Revenue vs Popularity and Budget vs Popularity

Notice. Since I will be using the revenue and budget variables for this part, I will use the horror_clean data set. This means some observation movies will be removed due to missing or zero values. Although this reduces the amount of data, I believe it helps ensure that the analysis is more accurate and not affected by incomplete values.

ggplot(horror_clean,
       aes(x = revenue,
           y = popularity)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::label_dollar()) +
  labs(title = "Revenue vs Popularity",
       x = "Revenue",
       y = "Popularity") +
  theme_minimal()
ggplot(horror_clean,
       aes(x = budget,
           y = popularity)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::label_dollar()) +
  labs(title = "Budget vs Popularity",
       x = "Budget",
       y = "Popularity") +
  theme_minimal()

From the two plots above, there are almost no relationship between revenue and popularity, and budget and popularity. Most movies are clustered at low popularity values, even when their revenue or budget is high, and the trend line appears almost flat.

Conclusion

From the observations above, we can see that horror movie success comes from a mix of factors. Higher budgets can help boost financial success, but they do not guarantee profit and popularity, Talk to Me proves that. High ratings do not always mean a movie is popular, and revenue does not always match popularity either. So, a movie’s success depends on more than just budget and popularity.

Footnotes

  1. Talk to Me https://www.rottentomatoes.com/m/talk_to_me_2023↩︎

  2. Talk to Me (2022) https://www.imdb.com/title/tt10638522/awards↩︎

  3. fmt_currency: Format values as currencies https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/gt/versions/1.2.0/topics/fmt_currency↩︎

  4. Position scales for continuous data (x & y) https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/scale_continuous.html↩︎
